firmware.name: LinuxCNC
firmware.description: Full-featured open-source CNC control system based on the NIST RS274/NGC standard. Runs on Linux with a real-time kernel. The reference implementation that grbl and grblHAL derive from.

codes.name: Rapid Move
codes.description: Rapid uncoordinated point-to-point motion. Moves each axis at its maximum rate. The path between start and end is NOT guaranteed to be a straight line. Supports up to 9 axes (XYZABCUVW).

examples.description: Rapid to origin at safe Z height
Rapid to position with rotary axis move

crossReferences.note: grbl implements the same NIST behavior but supports only XYZ axes.
Smoothieware treats G0 identically but uses separately configurable per-axis max rates.
In RepRapFirmware, G0 and G1 are identical — both use the F parameter. In LinuxCNC, G0 always uses maximum traverse rate.

modeNotes.description: LinuxCNC can blend consecutive rapid moves when G64 path blending is active, rounding corners within the specified tolerance. grbl and grblHAL do not support path blending.

codes.notes: LinuxCNC supports up to 9 axes (XYZABCUVW). Rapids may be affected by G64 path blending mode. The traverse rate is set per-axis in the INI file.

codes.name: Linear Move
codes.description: Coordinated linear move at the programmed feed rate. All axes arrive at the destination simultaneously. The tool path is always a precise straight line.

parameters.name: F
parameters.description: Feed rate (units per minute, or units per revolution with G95)

examples.description: Linear cut to X50 Y30 at 0.5mm depth, 500mm/min
Coordinated linear and rotary move

crossReferences.note: Same behavior. grbl limited to XYZ axes only.
RepRapFirmware uses S parameter on G1 for laser power. LinuxCNC does not support inline laser power on G1.

modeNotes.description: When G95 is active, the F word specifies units per spindle revolution instead of units per minute. Requires a spindle encoder.

codes.notes: The NIST reference implementation. grbl's G1 behavior is derived from this. Feed rate mode is controlled by G93/G94/G95.

codes.name: Clockwise Arc
codes.description: Clockwise circular or helical interpolation in the currently selected plane (G17/G18/G19). Supports both center-format (IJK) and radius-format (R). Unique to LinuxCNC: supports the P word for multi-turn arcs.

parameters.name: I
parameters.description: Arc center offset in X (incremental or absolute depending on G90.1/G91.1)

parameters.name: J
parameters.description: Arc center offset in Y (incremental or absolute depending on G90.1/G91.1)

parameters.name: K
parameters.description: Arc center offset in Z (incremental or absolute depending on G90.1/G91.1)

parameters.name: R
parameters.description: Arc radius (alternative to IJK; positive = minor arc, negative = major arc)

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: Number of full turns for multi-turn arcs (integer, unique to LinuxCNC)

examples.description: CW arc to X10 Y10 with center offset I5 J0
CW arc to X10 Y10 with radius 5 (minor arc)

examples.code: G2 X0 Y0 I5 J0 P3 F100
examples.description: Three full CW circles returning to start (multi-turn)

crossReferences.note: grbl supports the same IJK and R formats but does NOT support the P word for multi-turn arcs. grbl only supports incremental IJK (G91.1).
grblHAL adds P word support for multi-turn arcs, matching LinuxCNC behavior.
Smoothieware supports IJK and R formats. No P word for multi-turn arcs.

modeNotes.description: IJK values are absolute coordinates of the arc center, not offsets from the current position. This mode is unique to LinuxCNC among common CNC firmwares.

codes.notes: The P word for multi-turn arcs is unique to LinuxCNC (and grblHAL). A full circle requires the endpoint to equal the start point when using IJK format. Both G90.1 (absolute) and G91.1 (incremental) arc center modes are supported.

codes.name: Counter-Clockwise Arc
codes.description: Counter-clockwise circular or helical interpolation. Same parameters and behavior as G2 but in the opposite direction. Supports P word for multi-turn arcs.

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: Number of full turns for multi-turn arcs (integer)

examples.description: CCW arc to X10 Y10 with radius 5
Two full CCW circles (endpoint equals start)

crossReferences.note: Same base behavior. No P word support, no G90.1 absolute arc mode.
grblHAL supports P word for multi-turn arcs.

codes.name: Dwell
codes.description: Pauses program execution for a specified duration. The P parameter is in SECONDS (floating point). Non-modal.

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: Dwell time in seconds (float). P1.5 = 1.5 seconds

examples.description: Pause for 1 second
Pause for 500 milliseconds

crossReferences.note: grbl also uses P in seconds. Same behavior.
CONFLICT: Smoothieware interprets P as milliseconds (P1000 = 1 second) but also supports S in seconds.
CONFLICT: RepRapFirmware interprets P as milliseconds. P1000 = 1 second. Also supports S in seconds.
grblHAL uses P in seconds, same as LinuxCNC.

codes.notes: WARNING: P is in SECONDS in LinuxCNC, grbl, and grblHAL. In Smoothieware and RepRapFirmware, P is in MILLISECONDS. This is a common source of errors when porting G-code between CNC and 3D printing firmwares.

codes.name: Cubic Spline
codes.description: Cubic B-spline interpolation in the XY plane. Produces smooth curves using control points. Unique to LinuxCNC — not available in grbl, grblHAL, Smoothieware, or RepRapFirmware.

parameters.name: I
parameters.description: X offset of first control point from start

parameters.name: J
parameters.description: Y offset of first control point from start

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: X offset of second control point from end

parameters.name: Q
parameters.description: Y offset of second control point from end

examples.code: G5 I0 J3 P0 Q-3 X10 Y0 F200
examples.description: Cubic spline from current position to X10 Y0 with control points

crossReferences.note: Not supported. grbl has no spline interpolation.
Not supported. grblHAL has no spline interpolation.
RepRapFirmware supports G5 for Bezier splines but with different parameter semantics (uses I J P Q differently).

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC. The spline is defined by two control points: the first relative to the start point (I, J) and the second relative to the end point (P, Q). For consecutive splines, the second G5 can omit I and J to automatically mirror the previous endpoint's control point for smooth continuity.

codes.name: Quadratic Spline
codes.description: Quadratic B-spline interpolation in the XY plane. Uses a single control point. Unique to LinuxCNC.

parameters.name: I
parameters.description: X offset of control point from current position

parameters.name: J
parameters.description: Y offset of control point from current position

examples.description: Quadratic spline to X10 Y0 with control point offset I0 J5

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC. Simpler than G5 cubic splines — uses one control point instead of two. Useful for CAM-generated smooth curves.

codes.name: Lathe Diameter Mode
codes.description: X axis values are interpreted as diameters. A command of X2 moves the tool to a radius of 1 (diameter of 2). Only applies to lathe configurations.

examples.description: Turn to 25.4mm diameter

crossReferences.note: Not supported. grbl has no lathe mode.
Not supported. grblHAL has no diameter/radius mode.

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC. Only relevant for lathe configurations. Most lathe G-code uses diameter mode as it matches the measurement conventions used in turning (you measure the diameter of the part, not the radius).

codes.name: Lathe Radius Mode
codes.description: X axis values are interpreted as radii (actual tool position). A command of X1 moves the tool to a radius of 1 (diameter of 2). Only applies to lathe configurations.

examples.description: Turn to 12.7mm radius (25.4mm diameter)

crossReferences.note: Not supported. grbl has no lathe mode.

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC. In radius mode, X values directly correspond to the tool's distance from the spindle center.

codes.name: Set Coordinate System / Tool Offset Data
codes.description: Sets work coordinate system offsets or tool offsets. Used with an L word: L1/L10/L11 set tool offsets, L2 sets absolute coordinate offsets, L20 sets relative coordinate offsets. Uniquely supports the R parameter for coordinate system rotation.

parameters.name: L
parameters.description: Operation type: L1 = set tool offset (table), L2 = set coordinate offset (absolute), L10 = set tool offset (calculated), L11 = set tool offset (calculated, from G59.3), L20 = set coordinate offset (relative to current position)

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: Tool number (for L1/L10/L11) or coordinate system number 1-9 (for L2/L20, mapping to G54-G59.3)

parameters.name: R
parameters.description: Rotation angle in degrees about the Z axis (L2/L20 only, unique to LinuxCNC)

examples.code: G10 L2 P1 X10 Y20 Z0
examples.description: Set G54 offset to absolute values X10 Y20 Z0

examples.code: G10 L20 P1 X0 Y0 Z0
examples.description: Set G54 so current position becomes X0 Y0 Z0
Rotate G54 coordinate system 45 degrees about Z
Set tool 1 Z offset to -25.4 in tool table

crossReferences.note: grbl supports only L2 and L20 (coordinate offsets), with P1-P6 (G54-G59). No tool offset support or R rotation parameter.
grblHAL supports the same L words as LinuxCNC but does not support the R parameter for coordinate system rotation.
RepRapFirmware overloads G10: without parameters it performs firmware retract; with L/P parameters it sets offsets. No R rotation support.
Smoothieware uses bare G10 for firmware retract only. Coordinate offsets use G10 L2/L20 as separate commands. No R rotation support.

codes.notes: The R parameter for coordinate system rotation is unique to LinuxCNC. Supports 9 coordinate systems (P1-P9 mapping to G54-G59.3). When P0 is used with L2/L20, it applies to the currently active coordinate system.

codes.name: Set Coordinate System (Absolute)
codes.description: Sets a work coordinate system offset to absolute values. LinuxCNC uniquely supports the R parameter for coordinate system rotation.

parameters.name: R
parameters.description: Rotation angle in degrees about the Z axis (unique to LinuxCNC)

examples.code: G10 L2 P1 X10 Y20 Z0
examples.description: Set G54 offset to X10 Y20 Z0
Rotate G54 coordinate system 45 degrees about Z (unique)

crossReferences.note: Same base behavior but grbl does NOT support the R parameter for rotation, and only supports P1-P6 (G54-G59).
grblHAL supports P1-P6. No R rotation parameter.

codes.notes: The R parameter for coordinate system rotation is unique to LinuxCNC. Supports 9 coordinate systems (P1-P9 mapping to G54-G59.3). When P0 is used, it applies to the currently active coordinate system.

codes.name: Set Coordinate System (Relative)
codes.description: Sets a work coordinate system offset such that the current position becomes the specified coordinate value.

parameters.name: X
parameters.description: Desired X position at current location

parameters.name: Y
parameters.description: Desired Y position at current location

parameters.name: Z
parameters.description: Desired Z position at current location

parameters.name: A
parameters.description: Desired A position at current location

parameters.name: B
parameters.description: Desired B position at current location

parameters.name: C
parameters.description: Desired C position at current location

examples.code: G10 L20 P1 X0 Y0 Z0
examples.description: Set G54 so current position reads as X0 Y0 Z0
Set G59.1 so current XY reads as zero

crossReferences.note: Identical behavior per NIST standard. grbl limited to P1-P6.

codes.notes: Commonly used for setting work zero at the current position. Supports all 9 coordinate systems (P1-P9).

codes.name: XY Plane Select
codes.description: Selects XY as the active plane for arc commands (G2/G3), cutter compensation (G41/G42), and canned cycles. Default plane.

examples.description: CW arc in XY plane

crossReferences.note: Identical. LinuxCNC also supports G17.1 (UW plane) not available in grbl.

codes.name: ZX Plane Select
codes.description: Selects ZX as the active plane for arc commands. I and K specify arc center offsets. Commonly used in lathe operations.

examples.description: CW arc in ZX plane (lathe facing)

codes.name: YZ Plane Select
codes.description: Selects YZ as the active plane for arc commands. J and K specify arc center offsets.

examples.description: CCW arc in YZ plane

codes.name: Inch Mode
codes.description: Interprets all coordinate and feed rate values in inches.

examples.description: Move to X=1 inch at 10 inches/min

codes.name: Millimeter Mode
codes.description: Interprets all coordinate and feed rate values in millimeters.

examples.description: Move to X=10mm at 200mm/min

codes.name: Go to Predefined Position
codes.description: Makes a rapid traverse to the predefined position stored by G28.1. If intermediate coordinates are given, moves there first as a rapid, then to the stored position. This is NOT homing.

examples.description: Rapid to Z0, then to stored home position
Rapid directly to stored home position

crossReferences.note: Identical behavior — both follow the NIST standard.
WARNING: Smoothieware uses G28 for HOMING (finding limit switches), not for moving to a stored position. This is a critical incompatibility that can cause machine crashes.
WARNING: RepRapFirmware uses G28 for HOMING via macro files, not for moving to a stored position. Critical incompatibility.

codes.notes: CRITICAL: Per NIST standard, G28 is NOT homing. It moves to a stored position set by G28.1. LinuxCNC handles homing through the GUI/HAL interface, not via G-code. 3D printing firmwares (Smoothieware, RepRapFirmware) repurpose G28 as a homing command — never share G-code containing G28 between these platforms without review.

codes.name: Set Predefined Position 1
codes.description: Stores the current absolute position as the target for G28.

examples.description: Save current position as G28 target

codes.name: Go to Predefined Position 2
codes.description: Same as G28 but uses a second stored position (set by G30.1). Useful for tool change positions or secondary park locations.

examples.description: Move to stored G30 position
Move Z to 0 first, then to stored G30 position

crossReferences.note: WARNING: In Smoothieware, G30 is a single-point Z-probe command, NOT a move to a stored position.

codes.name: Set Predefined Position 2
codes.description: Stores the current absolute position as the target for G30.

examples.description: Save current position as G30 target

codes.name: Spindle Synchronized Motion
codes.description: Performs linear motion synchronized to the spindle rotation. Used for single-point threading on a lathe. Requires a spindle encoder. The K parameter specifies the distance per revolution (thread pitch).

examples.description: Cut a 1.5mm pitch thread to Z-20
Cut a thread with 0.05 inch pitch (20 TPI)

crossReferences.note: Not supported. grbl has no spindle synchronization.
grblHAL supports G33 with spindle encoder feedback.

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC (and grblHAL). Requires a spindle encoder connected via HAL. The spindle must be running before issuing G33. Feed rate is determined by spindle speed and K pitch, not by the F word. Multiple passes at the same K value will track the same thread groove.

codes.name: Rigid Tapping
codes.description: Performs a rigid tapping cycle synchronized to the spindle. The spindle reverses at the bottom of the hole to retract. Requires a spindle encoder.

examples.description: Rigid tap to Z-15 with 1.25mm pitch (M8x1.25)

crossReferences.note: Not supported. grbl has no spindle synchronization.
grblHAL supports rigid tapping with spindle encoder.

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC (and grblHAL). The spindle automatically reverses direction at the bottom to withdraw the tap. Requires precise spindle encoder feedback. Feed and speed overrides are disabled during the tapping cycle.

codes.name: Probe Toward Workpiece (Error on No Contact)
codes.description: Linear probe move toward workpiece. Stops on contact and records the position. Errors if no contact is made before reaching the target position.

examples.description: Probe downward to Z-10 at 50mm/min; error if no contact

codes.notes: Probe result is stored in parameters #5061-#5069 (probe coordinates) and #5070 (probe success flag). Use with O-word subroutines for automated probing routines.

codes.name: Probe Toward Workpiece (No Error)
codes.description: Same as G38.2 but does not error on failure. The probe success flag (#5070) indicates whether contact was made.

examples.description: Probe downward; no error if contact missed

codes.name: Probe Away from Workpiece (Error on No Contact)
codes.description: Probe expects to start in contact and moves away. Errors if the probe is still in contact at the target position.

examples.description: Probe upward away from surface; error if still in contact at Z5

codes.name: Probe Away from Workpiece (No Error)
codes.description: Same as G38.4 but does not error on failure.

examples.description: Probe away from surface; no error if still in contact

codes.name: Cancel Cutter Compensation
codes.description: Cancels cutter radius compensation (G41/G42). Returns to programming the tool center path. Default state.

examples.description: Cancel cutter compensation

crossReferences.note: grbl accepts G40 but does NOT support G41/G42 cutter compensation. G40 is the only supported mode.
grblHAL also only supports G40 — no cutter compensation.

codes.notes: LinuxCNC is one of the few open-source controllers that implements full cutter compensation. grbl and grblHAL do not support G41/G42.

codes.name: Cutter Compensation Left
codes.description: Activates cutter radius compensation to the left of the programmed path (conventional milling, climb direction). The controller automatically offsets the tool path by the tool radius. Tool radius comes from the tool table or is specified with D.

parameters.name: D
parameters.description: Tool number to use for compensation radius (from tool table). If omitted, uses the current tool.

examples.description: Enable left cutter comp using tool 1's radius, feed to X10
Enable left cutter comp using current tool's radius

crossReferences.note: Not supported. grbl does not implement cutter compensation. CAM must handle tool offsets.

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC among common open-source CNC controllers. Requires a lead-in move to ramp into compensation. The compensated path must not gouge the workpiece — LinuxCNC will error if the geometry is impossible. Very useful for manual G-code programming where the part geometry is programmed directly and the tool radius is applied by the controller.

codes.name: Cutter Compensation Right
codes.description: Activates cutter radius compensation to the right of the programmed path (climb milling, conventional direction). Same as G41 but offsets in the opposite direction.

parameters.name: D
parameters.description: Tool number to use for compensation radius (from tool table)

examples.description: Enable right cutter comp using tool 1's radius

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC. Same lead-in and geometry requirements as G41.

codes.name: Tool Length Offset (from Tool Table)
codes.description: Applies a tool length offset from the tool table. The H word selects which tool table entry to use.

parameters.name: H
parameters.description: Tool table entry number to use for the offset. H0 cancels the offset.

examples.description: Apply tool length offset from tool table entry 1
Cancel tool length offset (same as G49)

crossReferences.note: Not supported. grbl has no tool table. Use G43.1 for dynamic offsets.
grblHAL supports G43 with its tool table.

codes.notes: Requires a configured tool table. The tool table stores tool length, diameter, and other geometry. H typically matches the tool number but does not have to.

codes.name: Dynamic Tool Length Offset
codes.description: Applies a tool length offset directly from the G-code command, without using the tool table. Useful when the tool table is not available or for probed tool lengths.

parameters.name: Z
parameters.description: Tool length offset value (or whichever axis is configured as the tool axis)

examples.description: Apply -5mm tool length offset directly

crossReferences.note: grbl only supports G43.1 (dynamic). No tool table support.
grblHAL supports both G43 and G43.1.

codes.name: Tool Length Offset (Stacked)
codes.description: Applies an additional tool length offset that stacks on top of the current tool length offset. Unique to LinuxCNC.

parameters.name: H
parameters.description: Tool table entry number for the additional offset

examples.description: Apply tool 1 offset, then stack tool 10 offset on top

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC. Useful for adding a wear offset on top of a base tool length offset. The total offset is the sum of the current offset plus the H entry's offset.

codes.name: Cancel Tool Length Offset
codes.description: Cancels any active tool length offset (G43, G43.1, or G43.2). Default state.

examples.description: Cancel tool length offset

codes.name: Local Coordinate Offset
codes.description: Applies a temporary local coordinate offset on top of the active work coordinate system. Unlike G92, G52 is relative to the current WCS origin, not the current position. Setting all axes to zero cancels the offset.

examples.description: Shift the active WCS origin by X10 Y10
Cancel the local coordinate offset

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC. Useful for fixture sub-offsets — for example, when machining multiple identical parts in a fixture, use G54 for the fixture origin and G52 to offset to each part location.

codes.name: Move in Machine Coordinates
codes.description: Overrides the active work coordinate system for the current line only. Must be paired with G0 or G1 on the same line. Non-modal.

examples.description: Rapid to machine origin
Rapid Z to machine zero (top of travel)

crossReferences.note: Same behavior. Must be on same line as G0 or G1.

codes.notes: Non-modal — only applies to the line it appears on. The machine must be homed for G53 to work correctly.

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 1
codes.description: Selects work coordinate system 1. This is the default coordinate system.

examples.description: Switch to work coordinate system 1

crossReferences.note: Identical. grbl supports G54-G59 (6 systems). LinuxCNC supports G54-G59.3 (9 systems).

codes.notes: Default coordinate system. LinuxCNC supports 9 work coordinate systems (G54-G59.3), more than grbl's 6 (G54-G59).

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 2
codes.description: Selects work coordinate system 2.

examples.description: Switch to WCS 2

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 3
codes.description: Selects work coordinate system 3.

examples.description: Switch to WCS 3

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 4
codes.description: Selects work coordinate system 4.

examples.description: Switch to WCS 4

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 5
codes.description: Selects work coordinate system 5.

examples.description: Switch to WCS 5

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 6
codes.description: Selects work coordinate system 6.

examples.description: Switch to WCS 6

crossReferences.note: Identical. This is grbl's last WCS. LinuxCNC continues with G59.1-G59.3.

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 7
codes.description: Selects work coordinate system 7. Not available in grbl.

examples.description: Switch to WCS 7

crossReferences.note: Not supported. grbl only has 6 work coordinate systems (G54-G59).

codes.notes: Part of LinuxCNC's extended 9-WCS system. Set via G10 L2 P7 or G10 L20 P7.

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 8
codes.description: Selects work coordinate system 8. Not available in grbl.

examples.description: Switch to WCS 8

codes.notes: Set via G10 L2 P8 or G10 L20 P8.

codes.name: Work Coordinate System 9
codes.description: Selects work coordinate system 9. Not available in grbl.

examples.description: Switch to WCS 9

codes.notes: Set via G10 L2 P9 or G10 L20 P9.

codes.name: Exact Path Mode
codes.description: The controlled point follows the exact programmed path. The machine will not round corners but will decelerate and follow the exact corner. Different from G61.1 in that it does not necessarily stop at each corner.

examples.description: Enable exact path mode

crossReferences.note: grbl only supports G61 exact stop mode. No G64 path blending.

codes.notes: One of three path control modes in LinuxCNC: G61 (exact path), G61.1 (exact stop), and G64 (path blending). grbl only supports G61.

codes.name: Exact Stop Mode
codes.description: Machine decelerates to a complete stop at the end of each programmed move before executing the next. Produces the most accurate path but slowest cycle time.

examples.description: Enable exact stop mode

crossReferences.note: grbl's G61 behaves like LinuxCNC's G61.1 (full stop at each corner).

codes.notes: Slowest path mode. Use for maximum accuracy where corner rounding is unacceptable.

codes.name: Path Blending
codes.description: Enables path blending (continuous mode) where the controller is allowed to deviate from the exact programmed path by a specified tolerance to maintain feed rate. This is a critical unique feature of LinuxCNC that dramatically improves cycle times and surface finish.

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: Path blending tolerance (maximum deviation from programmed path in current units). Without P, the controller uses the best tolerance it can.

parameters.name: Q
parameters.description: Naive CAM tolerance — collinear segments within Q tolerance are merged. Eliminates stuttering from overly-segmented CAM output.

examples.description: Enable path blending with default tolerance (best effort)
Enable path blending with 0.01mm maximum deviation
Path blending with 0.01mm tolerance and 0.005mm naive CAM filter

crossReferences.note: Not supported. grbl uses $11 junction deviation setting for corner behavior, which is much less capable than G64.
Not supported. grblHAL uses junction deviation like grbl.
Not supported. Smoothieware uses junction deviation.
Not supported. RepRapFirmware uses its own motion planning with jerk limits.

codes.notes: CRITICAL unique feature of LinuxCNC. G64 P-Q- path blending is the single most important performance feature that separates LinuxCNC from microcontroller-based controllers. The P tolerance controls how much the tool can deviate from sharp corners. The Q naive CAM detector merges near-collinear segments, dramatically improving performance with high-polygon-count CAM output. Most LinuxCNC programs should start with G64 P0.01 or similar.

codes.name: Peck Drill Cycle (Chip Break)
codes.description: Canned drilling cycle with chip breaking. Drills in pecks but does NOT fully retract between pecks — only retracts a small amount (the chip break distance) to break the chip. Faster than G83 full-retract peck drilling.

parameters.name: R
parameters.description: Retract plane (rapid traverse to this Z before drilling)

examples.description: Peck drill at X10 Y10 to Z-20, 5mm pecks with chip break retract

crossReferences.note: Not supported. grbl has no canned drilling cycles.

modeNotes.description: G98 retracts to the initial Z level after each hole. G99 retracts to the R plane.

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC and grblHAL. Not in grbl or Smoothieware. The chip-break retract distance is controlled by the INI file. G73 is faster than G83 (full retract peck) because it only retracts enough to snap the chip.

codes.name: Threading Cycle
codes.description: Automated multi-pass threading cycle for lathes. Performs multiple spring passes and automatically calculates depth of cut using a compound slide angle. Requires spindle encoder.

parameters.name: I
parameters.description: Thread peak offset from Z start (sets first cutting depth, negative for external threads)

parameters.name: K
parameters.description: Full thread depth (positive, measured from thread peak to root)

parameters.name: R
parameters.description: Depth degression (1.0 = constant depth, 2.0 = constant area)

parameters.name: Q
parameters.description: Compound slide angle in degrees (29.5 for UN, 30 for metric)

parameters.name: H
parameters.description: Number of spring passes at final depth

examples.description: Cut M10x1.5 external thread to Z-20

crossReferences.note: Not supported. grbl has no threading capability.
grblHAL supports G76 with spindle encoder.

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC (and grblHAL). A powerful canned cycle that handles the complexity of multi-pass threading automatically. The degression parameter R controls whether successive passes remove constant depth or constant area of material.

codes.name: Cancel Canned Cycle
codes.description: Cancels the active canned cycle (G73, G76, G81-G89). Returns to normal motion mode. Also cancels the motion mode (no axis motion until a new G0/G1/G2/G3 is issued).

examples.description: Cancel canned cycle

crossReferences.note: In grbl, G80 cancels the motion mode. Same concept but grbl has no canned cycles to cancel.

codes.name: Drilling Cycle
codes.description: Basic drilling canned cycle. Rapids to R plane, feeds to depth Z, then rapids out. No dwell at bottom.

examples.description: Drill at X10 Y10 to Z-5, retract plane at Z2
Drill 3 holes at the same location

crossReferences.note: Not supported. grbl has no canned cycles.

modeNotes.description: G98 retracts to initial Z after each hole. G99 retracts to R plane.

codes.notes: Canned cycles are modal — once set, subsequent lines with XY coordinates will drill additional holes. Cancel with G80.

codes.name: Drilling Cycle with Dwell
codes.description: Same as G81 but dwells at the bottom of the hole before retracting. The dwell allows the drill to clean the bottom of the hole.

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: Dwell time in seconds at bottom of hole

examples.description: Drill with 0.5 second dwell at bottom

codes.name: Peck Drill Cycle (Full Retract)
codes.description: Deep-hole peck drilling cycle. Drills in increments, fully retracting to the R plane between each peck to clear chips. Slower than G73 but more effective for deep holes and gummy materials.

examples.description: Peck drill to Z-30 in 5mm pecks with full retract

codes.name: Right-Hand Tapping Cycle
codes.description: Canned tapping cycle. Feeds to depth, then reverses spindle and feeds out. For use with a floating tap holder (not rigid tapping — use G33.1 for rigid tapping).

parameters.name: F
parameters.description: Feed rate (must match spindle speed and tap pitch)

examples.description: Tap at X10 Y10 to Z-15

codes.name: Boring Cycle (Feed Out)
codes.description: Canned boring cycle. Feeds to depth Z, then feeds back out at the programmed feed rate (does not rapid retract). Produces a better finish than G81 on the way out.

examples.description: Bore to Z-5 and feed out at same feed rate

codes.name: Boring Cycle (Spindle Stop, Rapid Out)
codes.description: Canned boring cycle. Feeds to depth, stops spindle, then rapids out. Prevents drag marks on the bore wall during retract.

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: Dwell time in seconds at bottom (optional)

examples.description: Bore to Z-5, stop spindle, rapid out

codes.name: Boring Cycle (Dwell, Feed Out)
codes.description: Canned boring cycle. Feeds to depth, dwells at bottom, then feeds back out. Combines G82 dwell with G85 feed-out retract.

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: Dwell time in seconds at bottom

examples.description: Bore to Z-5, dwell 1 second, feed out

codes.name: Absolute Distance Mode
codes.description: Coordinates interpreted as absolute positions relative to the active work coordinate system origin. Default mode per NIST standard.

examples.description: Set absolute distance mode

codes.notes: Default mode per NIST standard.

codes.name: Incremental Distance Mode
codes.description: Coordinates interpreted as relative distances from the current position.

examples.description: Move 10 units in X and Y from current position

codes.notes: Per NIST standard.

codes.name: Absolute Arc Distance Mode
codes.description: Arc center offsets (I, J, K) are interpreted as absolute coordinates of the arc center, not offsets from the current position. Unique to LinuxCNC among common CNC firmwares.

examples.description: CW arc where I5 J0 is the absolute center position

crossReferences.note: Not supported. grbl only supports G91.1 (incremental arc offsets).

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC. Most CAM software outputs incremental IJK (G91.1). Absolute arc mode is mainly useful for hand-written G-code where it can simplify the math.

codes.name: Incremental Arc Distance Mode
codes.description: Arc center offsets (I, J, K) are relative to the current position. This is the default and most common mode. All firmwares support this mode.

crossReferences.note: Identical. This is the only arc offset mode grbl supports.

codes.notes: Default mode. IJK values specify the arc center as an offset from the current position.

codes.name: Set Coordinate System Offset
codes.description: Sets a temporary coordinate offset so that the current position reads as the specified value. The offset applies on top of the active work coordinate system.

parameters.name: X
parameters.description: Desired X position at current location

parameters.name: Y
parameters.description: Desired Y position at current location

parameters.name: Z
parameters.description: Desired Z position at current location

parameters.name: A
parameters.description: Desired A position at current location

parameters.name: B
parameters.description: Desired B position at current location

parameters.name: C
parameters.description: Desired C position at current location

examples.description: Set current position as X0 Y0

crossReferences.note: Same behavior but grbl's G92 is non-persistent (cleared on reset). LinuxCNC can optionally persist G92 offsets (configurable in INI).

codes.notes: LinuxCNC can optionally persist G92 offsets between sessions (controlled by INI file setting). Prefer G10 L20 for setting work offsets. G92 applies on top of the active WCS.

codes.name: Clear G92 Offset
codes.description: Cancels the G92 offset, resetting it to zero. Also resets the offset in the variable file if persistence is enabled.

examples.description: Clear temporary coordinate offset

codes.name: Inverse Time Feed Rate Mode
codes.description: Feed rate F specifies the inverse of time in minutes to complete the move. Each motion command MUST include an F word. F2 means the move should complete in 0.5 minutes.

examples.description: Move to X10 in 0.5 minutes (F = 1/time_in_min)

codes.notes: Every motion line must include F in inverse time mode. Commonly used with 4th/5th axis work or complex multi-axis tool paths generated by CAM.

codes.name: Units Per Minute Feed Rate Mode
codes.description: Feed rate F is in units (mm or inches) per minute. This is the default mode.

examples.description: Move at 500 units/min

codes.name: Units Per Revolution Feed Rate Mode
codes.description: Feed rate F specifies units per spindle revolution. Requires a spindle encoder. Commonly used for lathe turning and drilling where consistent chip load per revolution is desired.

examples.description: Feed at 0.15mm per revolution

crossReferences.note: Not supported. grbl has no spindle encoder input.
grblHAL supports G95 with spindle encoder.

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC (and grblHAL). Essential for lathe operations. The actual feed rate in mm/min varies with spindle speed. Requires a spindle encoder connected via HAL.

codes.name: Constant Surface Speed Mode
codes.description: Spindle speed is specified as surface speed (units per minute) rather than RPM. The controller automatically adjusts RPM as the tool moves closer to or farther from the spindle center. Essential for lathe turning to maintain consistent cutting conditions.

parameters.name: S
parameters.description: Surface speed in units per minute

parameters.name: D
parameters.description: Maximum spindle RPM (to prevent overspeed near center)

examples.description: CSS at 100m/min surface speed, max 3000 RPM

crossReferences.note: Not supported. grbl has no CSS mode.

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC. Critical for lathe turning — produces consistent surface finish regardless of part diameter. The D parameter sets a maximum RPM limit to prevent the spindle from exceeding safe speed as the tool approaches the center of the part.

codes.name: RPM Mode (Constant RPM)
codes.description: Spindle speed is specified directly in RPM. This is the default spindle mode. Cancels G96 constant surface speed mode.

examples.description: Set spindle to 1500 RPM, start clockwise

crossReferences.note: grbl always operates in RPM mode (no G96/G97 commands needed).

codes.notes: Default spindle mode. Use G97 to explicitly return to RPM mode after using G96 CSS mode.

codes.name: Canned Cycle Initial Level Return
codes.description: After each canned cycle repetition, retract to the initial Z level (the Z position when the canned cycle was first invoked). Default retract mode for canned cycles.

examples.description: Drill and retract to initial Z (above R plane)

crossReferences.note: Not supported. grbl has no canned cycles.

codes.notes: Useful when obstacles exist between holes that are above the R plane. The tool retracts higher to clear them.

codes.name: Canned Cycle R-Level Return
codes.description: After each canned cycle repetition, retract only to the R plane. Faster than G98 when there are no obstacles between holes.

examples.description: Drill and retract to R plane (Z2)

codes.notes: Faster cycle times than G98 since the retract distance is shorter.

codes.name: Program Pause
codes.description: Halts program execution unconditionally. The operator must press the cycle start button to resume. Spindle and coolant remain in their current state.

examples.description: Pause program — wait for operator to resume

codes.name: Optional Program Pause
codes.description: Pauses program execution only if the optional stop switch is active in the LinuxCNC GUI. If the switch is off, M1 is ignored.

examples.description: Optional pause — only if operator enabled optional stop

crossReferences.note: In grbl, M1 always pauses (same as M0) because there is no optional stop switch.

codes.notes: The optional stop switch is available in the LinuxCNC GUI. When disabled, M1 lines are skipped entirely.

codes.name: Program End
codes.description: Ends the program. Stops spindle and coolant, resets modal states to defaults, and positions the program back to the top.

examples.description: End program

codes.name: Program End and Rewind
codes.description: Ends the program, stops spindle and coolant, and rewinds to the beginning. Functionally equivalent to M2 in LinuxCNC.

examples.description: End program and rewind

codes.name: Spindle On (CW)
codes.description: Starts the spindle rotating clockwise at the speed specified by S. LinuxCNC uses HAL pins for spindle control, making it highly configurable. Supports multi-spindle configurations using the $ prefix.

examples.description: Start spindle CW at 18000 RPM
Start spindle 1 CW at 5000 RPM (multi-spindle)

crossReferences.note: Same base behavior. In grbl laser mode ($32=1), M3 enables constant laser power. LinuxCNC does not have a built-in laser mode.
Same behavior. grblHAL extends S value range.
Smoothieware does not differentiate M3/M4 for laser mode.

modeNotes.description: When G96 is active, the S value specifies surface speed, not RPM. The controller calculates the required RPM based on the current X position.

codes.notes: Spindle control in LinuxCNC is highly configurable through HAL. Multi-spindle support via $ prefix is unique to LinuxCNC. Does not include grbl-style laser mode — laser control requires custom HAL configuration.

codes.name: Spindle On (CCW)
codes.description: Starts the spindle rotating counter-clockwise at the speed specified by S. Supports multi-spindle configurations.

examples.description: Start spindle CCW at 12000 RPM

crossReferences.note: In grbl laser mode ($32=1), M4 enables dynamic laser power that scales with speed. In LinuxCNC, M4 is purely CCW spindle rotation.
Smoothieware does not differentiate M3/M4 for laser mode.

codes.notes: Strictly counter-clockwise rotation. Unlike grbl/grblHAL, M4 has no special laser dynamic power behavior.

codes.name: Spindle Off
codes.description: Stops all spindle rotation. Supports multi-spindle via $ prefix.

examples.description: Stop spindle
Stop spindle 1 only

codes.name: Tool Change
codes.description: Performs a tool change. The T word must have been previously issued to select the tool. LinuxCNC uses HAL for tool change signaling and supports manual, semi-automatic, and fully automatic tool changers.

parameters.name: T
parameters.description: Tool number (must be pre-selected with a T word before M6)

examples.description: Select tool 1 and execute tool change
Pre-select tool 3, then change

crossReferences.note: grbl does not support M6 tool changes. T word just sets the tool number.
grblHAL supports M6 with configurable tool change modes (manual, semi-auto).

codes.notes: T word pre-selects the tool (can be on the same line or a prior line). M6 executes the change. The tool change behavior is entirely configurable through HAL — from a simple pause for manual change to a fully automated carousel or rack system. G43 should typically follow M6 to apply the new tool's length offset.

codes.name: Mist Coolant On
codes.description: Enables mist coolant output via HAL pin.

examples.description: Enable mist coolant

crossReferences.note: Same behavior. Requires ENABLE_M7 compile-time option in grbl.

codes.name: Flood Coolant On
codes.description: Enables flood coolant output via HAL pin.

examples.description: Enable flood coolant

codes.name: All Coolant Off
codes.description: Disables both mist and flood coolant.

examples.description: Turn off all coolant

codes.name: Orient Spindle
codes.description: Rotates the spindle to a specific orientation angle and holds it there. Requires a spindle encoder or position feedback. Used for oriented tool changes, indexing, and machining operations that require a specific spindle position.

parameters.name: Q
parameters.description: Number of seconds to wait for orient to complete

examples.description: Orient spindle to 0 degrees
Orient spindle to 90 degrees, rotating CW

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC. Requires spindle orientation HAL pins to be connected. Commonly used before tool changes on machines with oriented tool holders, or for C-axis milling operations on lathes.

codes.name: Enable Feed and Speed Overrides
codes.description: Enables both feed rate and spindle speed overrides. This is the default state.

examples.description: Enable feed and speed overrides

crossReferences.note: grbl always allows overrides via real-time commands. No M48/M49 equivalent.

codes.name: Disable Feed and Speed Overrides
codes.description: Disables both feed rate and spindle speed overrides. The machine runs at the exact programmed rates regardless of the override knobs.

examples.description: Disable feed and speed overrides (lock to programmed values)

crossReferences.note: No equivalent. grbl overrides are always active via real-time commands.

codes.notes: Useful during critical operations like threading or tapping where speed changes could damage the workpiece or tooling.

codes.name: Feed Override Control
codes.description: Individually enables or disables feed rate overrides. When disabled, the feed rate is locked to the programmed value.

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: 0 = disable feed override, 1 = enable feed override

examples.description: Disable feed rate override
Enable feed rate override

crossReferences.note: Not supported. grbl has no per-override enable/disable.

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC. Provides finer control than M48/M49 which toggle all overrides at once.

codes.name: Spindle Speed Override Control
codes.description: Individually enables or disables spindle speed overrides.

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: 0 = disable spindle override, 1 = enable spindle override

examples.description: Disable spindle speed override
Enable spindle speed override

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC.

codes.name: Adaptive Feed Control
codes.description: Enables or disables adaptive feed. When enabled, the feed rate can be adjusted in real-time by a HAL pin (e.g., for power-based adaptive feed control).

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: 0 = disable adaptive feed, 1 = enable adaptive feed

examples.description: Enable adaptive feed control

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC. The adaptive-feed HAL pin can scale the feed rate from 0% to 100%. Commonly used with spindle load monitoring to automatically slow the feed when cutting hard spots.

codes.name: Feed Stop Control
codes.description: Enables or disables the feed stop HAL input. When enabled, an external input can pause motion while keeping the spindle running.

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: 0 = disable feed stop, 1 = enable feed stop

examples.description: Enable feed stop input

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC. Allows external hardware (e.g., a foot pedal) to control motion feed hold while keeping the spindle running.

codes.name: Set Current Tool Number
codes.description: Changes the current tool number in the controller without performing a physical tool change. Useful for syncing the controller's tool tracking with reality after a manual intervention.

parameters.name: Q
parameters.description: Tool number to set as current

examples.description: Set current tool to tool 3 without changing

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC. No physical tool change occurs. Only updates the internal tool tracking. Useful in recovery situations or when the tool was changed outside of program control.

codes.name: Digital Output On (Synchronized)
codes.description: Turns on a digital output synchronized with the start of the next motion command. The output change is queued and executes when the next motion begins.

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: Digital output number (0-3 maps to motion.digital-out-00 through -03)

examples.description: Turn on digital output 0 at the start of the move to X10

crossReferences.note: Not supported. grbl has no general-purpose I/O control via G-code.

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC. Synchronized I/O is crucial for operations like activating a laser or spray at the exact moment motion begins. The output is connected via HAL to any physical hardware.

codes.name: Digital Output Off (Synchronized)
codes.description: Turns off a digital output synchronized with the start of the next motion command.

examples.description: Turn off digital output 0 at start of next motion

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC.

codes.name: Digital Output On (Immediate)
codes.description: Turns on a digital output immediately, without waiting for the next motion command. The output changes as soon as the command is read.

examples.description: Immediately turn on digital output 0

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC. Unlike M62, this does not synchronize with motion. Use when timing relative to motion is not critical.

codes.name: Digital Output Off (Immediate)
codes.description: Turns off a digital output immediately, without waiting for motion.

examples.description: Immediately turn off digital output 0

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC.

codes.name: Wait on Input
codes.description: Waits for a digital or analog input to reach a specified condition. Can wait for a rising edge, falling edge, or specific level. The result is stored in parameter #5399.

parameters.name: E
parameters.description: Analog input number (0-3, use E instead of P for analog)

examples.description: Wait up to 5 seconds for digital input 0 to go high
Immediately read analog input 0 value into #5399

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC. Powerful for automation workflows — wait for a fixture clamp to close, a part to be loaded, or read a sensor value. Result is in #5399: for digital, 0 or 1; for analog, the actual value; -1 on timeout.

codes.name: Analog Output (Synchronized)
codes.description: Sets an analog output value synchronized with the start of the next motion command.

examples.description: Set analog output 0 to 0.75 at start of motion

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC. Useful for controlling laser power, spindle speed analog signals, or other variable outputs synchronized with motion.

codes.name: Analog Output (Immediate)
codes.description: Sets an analog output value immediately, without waiting for motion.

examples.description: Immediately set analog output 0 to 0.5

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC.

codes.name: Save Modal State
codes.description: Saves the current modal state (G-code modes, feed rate, spindle speed, tool, etc.) to a stack. Can be restored later with M72. Useful in subroutines and remap procedures.

examples.description: Save current modal state to stack

crossReferences.note: Not supported. grbl has no modal state save/restore.

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC. Works as a stack — multiple M70 calls push multiple states. Each M72 pops the most recent. Essential for writing robust subroutines that restore the caller's modal state.

codes.name: Restore Modal State
codes.description: Restores the modal state previously saved by M70. Pops the most recent state from the stack.

examples.description: Save state, do work, restore state

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC. Must be paired with a prior M70.

codes.name: Save and Autorestore Modal State
codes.description: Saves the current modal state and automatically restores it when the current subroutine or remap returns. No explicit M72 needed.

examples.code:
O100 sub
M73
(changes to modes are automatically restored on return)
O100 endsub
examples.description: Auto-restore modal state on subroutine exit

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC. More convenient than M70/M72 for subroutines since the restore is automatic. The state is restored even if the subroutine exits early.

codes.name: User Defined M-codes
codes.description: LinuxCNC supports user-defined M-codes in the range M100 through M199. Each executes an external program or script named M1xx (e.g., M101 runs the file named M101 in the NC files directory). The P and Q parameters are passed as command-line arguments.

parameters.name: P
parameters.description: First argument passed to the external program

parameters.name: Q
parameters.description: Second argument passed to the external program

examples.description: Run external program M101 with arguments P=5 Q=3
Run external program M100 with no arguments

crossReferences.note: Not supported. grbl cannot run external programs.
RepRapFirmware has a macro system but with different semantics (M98 calls macro files).

codes.notes: Unique to LinuxCNC. The external program can be any executable (shell script, Python, compiled binary). It receives P and Q as arguments. The program is searched for in the directory specified by [DISPLAY]PROGRAM_PREFIX in the INI file. Execution pauses until the external program completes.

codes.name: Define Subroutine
codes.description: Defines a reusable subroutine. Lines between 'sub' and 'endsub' are not executed until the subroutine is called. Subroutine bodies may not be nested. Maximum nesting level for calls is 10. Subroutines can optionally return a value via an expression on endsub or return.

examples.code:
o100 sub
  G53 G0 X0 Y0 Z0
o100 endsub

o100 call
examples.description: Define and call a simple subroutine

examples.code:
o<move_home> sub
  G53 G0 X0 Y0 Z0
o<move_home> endsub

o<move_home> call
examples.description: Named subroutine

examples.code:
o200 sub
o200 endsub [#1 * #2]
; return value in #<_value>
examples.description: Subroutine with return value

crossReferences.note: grblHAL implements LinuxCNC-compatible O-code subroutines. Same syntax.
RepRapFirmware uses M98 to call macro files instead of O-code subroutines.

codes.notes: O-codes can be numbered (o100) or named (o<myname>). Named codes are converted to lowercase. Use the letter 'O' (or 'o'), NOT the digit zero. Subroutines must be defined before they are called.

codes.name: Call Subroutine
codes.description: Calls a subroutine, optionally passing up to 30 arguments. Arguments are accessed as #1 through #30 inside the subroutine and are local — restored to pre-call values on return. Parameters above #30 are global and changes persist after return.

parameters.name: [arg1]...[argN]
parameters.description: Up to 30 arguments in square brackets

examples.description: Call subroutine 100 with no arguments
Call named subroutine (also searches for file myfile.ngc)
Computed O-number (indirection)

crossReferences.note: grblHAL implements compatible O-code call syntax.
RepRapFirmware uses M98 P"file.g" with param.<letter> for parameters.

codes.notes: Arguments MUST be in square brackets (e.g., [100] not 100). Named subroutines can call external files — the file is searched in PROGRAM_PREFIX or SUBROUTINE_PATH directories. Recursive calls are supported.

codes.name: Return from Subroutine
codes.description: Immediately returns to the calling code, as though endsub was encountered. May optionally return a value via an expression, which is stored in the predefined named parameter #<_value>.

examples.description: Return with no value
Return computed value (stored in #<_value>)

codes.notes: Return value stored in #<_value>, and #<_value_returned> is set to 1. Both are global and cleared before the next subroutine call.

codes.name: Conditional: if/elseif/else/endif
codes.description: Conditional execution of G-code blocks. Evaluates conditions in order until one is true. Supports if, elseif (optional, repeatable), else (optional), and endif. All must share the same O-number.

parameters.name: [condition]
parameters.description: Boolean expression using comparison operators (EQ, NE, GT, GE, LT, LE) and logical operators (AND, OR, XOR)

examples.code:
o101 if [#31 EQ 3]
  S2000
o101 endif
examples.description: Simple conditional

examples.code:
o102 if [#2 GT 5]
  F100
o102 elseif [#2 LT 2]
  F200
o102 else
  F150
o102 endif
examples.description: Full if/elseif/else/endif

crossReferences.note: grblHAL implements compatible O-code if/endif syntax.
RepRapFirmware uses indentation-based if/elif/else with object model expressions instead of O-numbers.
FluidNC does not support conditional G-code.

codes.notes: All branches (if/elseif/else/endif) must use the same O-number. Conditions use square brackets with comparison operators: EQ, NE, GT, GE, LT, LE, AND, OR, XOR.

codes.name: Loop: while/endwhile
codes.description: Executes a block of G-code repeatedly while a condition is true. Tests the condition first, then executes the body. The while and endwhile must share the same O-number.

examples.description: Draw sawtooth pattern

crossReferences.note: RepRapFirmware uses indentation-based while with 'iterations' counter.

codes.name: Loop: do/while
codes.description: Executes a block at least once, then repeats while the condition is true. The body executes before the condition is tested. The do and while must share the same O-number.

examples.code:
#1 = 0
o100 do
  (debug, parameter 1 = #1)
  #1 = [#1 + 1]
o100 while [#1 LT 3]
examples.description: Execute body at least once

codes.name: Loop: repeat/endrepeat
codes.description: Executes a block a specified number of times. The count is evaluated once at the start.

examples.code:
G91
o103 repeat [5]
  G1 X10 F500
  G0 X-10 Y5
o103 endrepeat
G90
examples.description: Mill 5 diagonal shapes

crossReferences.note: RepRapFirmware uses 'while iterations < N' for counted loops.

codes.name: Break (Exit Loop)
codes.description: Immediately exits the enclosing while, do/while, or repeat loop. The O-number must match the loop's O-number.

examples.code:
o100 while [1]
  o110 if [#1 GT 10]
    o100 break
  o110 endif
  #1 = [#1 + 1]
o100 endwhile
examples.description: Break out of while loop

codes.notes: The O-number on break must match the enclosing loop's O-number.

codes.name: Continue (Next Iteration)
codes.description: Skips the rest of the current loop body and jumps to the next evaluation of the loop condition. The O-number must match the enclosing loop's O-number.

examples.code:
o100 do
  o110 if [#1 EQ 2]
    #1 = 3
    o100 continue
  o110 endif
  #1 = [#1 + 1]
o100 while [#1 LT 3]
examples.description: Skip iteration when #1 equals 2
